entry.title: Re: Enable ssl in Hana replication for DR
entry.content:
Hi,

SSL is enabled by default.

So if you want to turn it on explicitly , you can set it online with command :

  • ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('global.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('system_replication_communication','enable_ssl') = 'on' WITH RECONFIGURE

Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: Re: Email Template not generating in Q-System after Softw...
entry.content:
Good day @arnap_silea16cc 

Thank you for your question.

A gap you might have after you have transported a custom email template with Export Software Collection and Import Collection is not a separate activation in Q, but the setup for your business relevant object and output type in Output Parameter Determination. A template can be visible in Maintain Email Templates and still not be used to generate email if you miss the required config in output management. To ensure that is not the case, please check these points first in Q:

  1. Assign Email Templates (ID 101945) to the business context. If the template was imported but not properly assigned to the relevant business context, it may appear in Maintain Email Templates but not be valid in Output Parameter Determination. See KBA 3359258 for config steps.                                                                                     
  2. Open Output Parameter Determination for the affected application object and output type, verify the channel is Email, your custom email template is selected and you have maintained your Sender email in Email Settings. See KBA 2676588 on Email Setting steps and its impacts.                                                                                       
  3. There is usually no separate activation step in Maintain Email Templates itself after you have completed the import. The relevant action is for you to run Check and Activate in Output Parameter Determination (in Q) if the rule is inconsistent or inactive. See KBA 2843926 for output rules.

So, in summary, the item commonly missed is the business context and OPD assignment, not the transport of the template object itself. The checks mentioned above should be carried out if the template is visible in Q but emails are no longer generated. 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP B1 GRPO posting wrong amount to Inventory acct fo...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

sorry, I focused on the highlighted row. Can you please tell me what documents PD2600147 and PD2600148 are? Seems to me those are cancellation documents (looking from Cumulative Qty column). If so, then the explanation is simple, Trans. Value is not showing the new item cost, but rather increase/decrease of inventory value happening with that transaction. To explain:

PD2500082 added to stock 21.000 value, which was removed with DN2500034

PD 2600109 needed to have avg. cost of 21.750, and since there was no qty on stock it achieved this by setting the value to that amount. This value remained as no items were sold (delivered) before the next GRPO

PD 2600133 by calculations had to increase the inventory value to a total of 44.000, and to achieve that it needed the difference 44.000 - 21.750 = 22.250 added to your stock value account (as shown in Trans Value).

Now, when you decided to cancel those GRPOs (PD2600147 and PD2600148), the system removed for each item its calculated value of 22.000 ultimately ending up with 0 stock value and 0 qty.

 

BR,

Matija

entry.title: Re: Observations on NSE Implementation for CDPOS Table in...
entry.content:
Hello,

Warm page loadable NSE data are loaded into these "warm" heap allocators in HANA when accessed :

  • Pool/CS/BufferPage
  • Pool/CS/BufferCachePageProvider:Controlblock
  • Pool/CS/BufferCachePageProvider:DataPage

While the table columns and pages themselves are present in the buffer cache, the fetch requests, Calculations and joins that are done on the CDPOS table on a statement level, will be populated under the "Statement & Intermediate Results" heap allocators like "Pool/JoinEvaluator/ . . " for example and thus these allocators are stored in "hot" main memory until the statement finishes.

The LOAD_UNIT column in the TABLE_COLUMNS view will show which of the CDPOS columns are stored in "PAGE" loadable units or not, since not every column may have been configured as page loadable. Any non-page loadable columns will be loaded into the hot memory by default under "Pool/Columnstore/ . . ." .

SQL: "HANA_Tables_ColumnStore_TableSize_2.00.030+" (SAP Note 1969700) reports the NSE-related memory consumption in column PAGED_GB.

See section '12. How can I calculate the memory consumption of NSE enabled tables?' - 2799997 - FAQ: SAP HANA Native Storage Extension (NSE)

You can remove the table from NSE at any time via command : ALTER TABLE "<table_name>" DEFAULT LOADABLE;

Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: How to Add Custom Field in Purchase Requisition Item in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (Step-by-Step)
entry.content:
Business Requirement

In one of my clients, there is a requirement where we need to capture Department information at the Purchase Requisition (PR) Item level for:

  • Internal tracking
  • Reporting & analytics

However, this field is not available as standard in SAP Public Cloud.

Solution

There is a tile called Custom Fields, where we can create a custom field, and then we can assign it to PR. The detailed steps are below:

Step 1: Create Custom Field

> Navigate to the Custom Fields App as shown below:

Create a new field as shown below :

  • Label: DEPARTMENT

  • Identifier: YY1_DEPARTMENT

  • Business Context:
    Procurement: Purchase Requisition Item (MM_PURREQN_ITEM)

  • Field Type: Code List

Here, we are choosing codel because it fulfils my requirement where we can assigned muliplte pre defined value, like how we did in the ECC & S/4 Hana on-prem when creating a domain:

Step 2: Maintain Code List Values

Define department values:

Code Description

ACCAccounts
AUDAudit
ENGEngineering
ITIT
MKT1Marketing-I
MKT2Marketing-II
SSStock & Store

This allows dropdown/value help in UI

Step 3: Maintain UI Texts

Configure labels properly:

  • Label: DEPARTMENT (Desc.)

  • Short Label: Dept.

  • Medium Label: Dept.D

This ensures proper display across UI screens.

Step 4: Enable Field Usage

Go to the User Interfaces tab and enable usage in:

CDS Views

  • C_EXTPURCHASEREQUISITIONITEM

  • I_PURCHASEREQUISITION_API01

  • I_PURCHASEREQUISITIONITEMAPI01

SAP GUI

  • PR_ITEM_GUI (Purchase Requisition Item GUI)

This is a very important step (most people miss this)

Step 5: Publish the Field

Click Publish

Once published:

  • Field is added to the database structure

  • Available for UI & API consumption

Step 6: Test in Purchase Requisition

Open Purchase Requisition screen.

New Tab Appears:

Department

Inside tab:

  • Field: Department

  • Value Help enabled

Behaviour in System

  • User selects department from the dropdown

  • Values are controlled via Code List

  • Field is stored at the PR Item level

Key Technical Points

  • Field is automatically extended in:

    • CDS Views

    • OData Services

    • UI annotations

  • No need for manual ABAP coding

  • Fully compliant with the SAP Public Cloud extensibility model

Advantages

✔️ No modification required
✔️ Works in both SAP GUI & Fiori
✔️ Reusable across APIs & reports
✔️ Easy maintenance via Code List

Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Not enabling CDS views
❌ Forgetting SAP GUI enablement
❌ Not publishing after changes
❌ Incorrect business context selection

Conclusion

Custom Fields in SAP Public Cloud provide a powerful, upgrade-safe way to enhance standard applications.

With minimal effort, we successfully:
✔️Added Department field
✔️Enabled dropdown values
✔️Integrated with UI & backend

This approach can be reused for multiple business requirements across procurement and finance.

Personal Experience:

If this requirement for ECC or S/4 Hana on-prem, we could have done it in the old traditional way. But this public cloud experience is on another level, the satisfaction I got while doing the research about it can be compared to how we used to search user exits and customer exits at first time when entering into SAP. Even tho SAP has restricted most things for public cloud, it also provides us an easy solution, as we can see it here. There is one more app called Custom Logic, where we can write code for the validation, which we will discuss later.

Please feel free to connect if you have any queries.

Happy Learning 😀

entry.title: When to Use Which Approach for Settlement of Assets Under Construction
entry.content:
Co-author: H. Trefftz

Managing assets under construction efficiently is crucial for accurate financial reporting.

Assets under construction are a separate balance sheet item in the company's fixed assets. In Asset Accounting, there are separate asset classes for assets under construction and assets under construction have their own asset master records.

Assets under construction (AuC) are temporary objects: Either the completed asset is not yet known, or the completed asset cannot yet be finally capitalized, for example, because it is still in the completion process.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, there are basically two approaches to how the settlement postings to and from an AuC are handled:

  • Summary settlement
  • Line item-based settlement

But which one should you choose? To help you make the right decision, let's break down the key differences and explain the advantages and disadvantages.

Summary Settlement

Characteristics

You have a WBS element with the project type Investment Project in Controlling. When releasing this investment project, a corresponding asset under construction is created in Asset Accounting. In Asset Accounting, you manually create one or more completed assets.

At first, the costs of the under construction phase are collected on the WBS element. There are two settlement steps:

  1. The periodic settlement from the WBS element to the AuC:
    The amounts that require capitalization are cleared during this periodic settlement and debited to the corresponding AuC.
  2. The final settlement of the AuC to the completed assets.

This settlement including periodic settlement from WBS element to AuC is done using a summary settlement.

Summary settlement is intended for the mass processing of postings that are to be settled to an AuC and to final receivers. In particular, if many (N) postings are to be settled to a large number (M) of receivers, such an N:M relationship is processed efficiently by a summary settlement.

Summary settlement always treats the amount to be settled as a balance and not at line item-level. Therefore, if there are many postings to an investment project, the periodic settlement from the WBS element to the AuC cumulates these amounts in a balance per settlement period and settles this balance to the AuC. A preliminary settlement rule can be used to settle partial amounts or percentages of the balance to receivers other than the AuC during periodic settlement.

If the final settlement rule contains a large number (M) of receiver objects, the amounts on the AuC that are to be settled again are balanced. We therefore have a 1:M relationship of sender (AuC) and receiver items.

This aggregation can be processed in a performance-optimized way for many postings within a settlement period. If you don't want to settle every fiscal period, you can decide to settle less frequently, for example, once a quarter.

However, due to the aggregation at AuC level, there is no reference between the original cost posting to the WBS element and the receiver item on the completed asset.

Figure 1 shows the summary settlement of an investment project: Per receiver asset (or other receiver of final settlement), the retirement on AuC is split by receiver (M credit lines); the acquisition on the receiver each creates a separate document line as well on debit side.

Figure 1: Summary Settlement of Investment Project (WBS Element)

Pros and Cons

These are the pros and cons of summary settlement.

Pros:

  • Efficient processing of high-volume postings to investment project (WBS element)
  • Efficient processing of settlement to many receiver assets
  • Amount-based settlement rule possible
  • Capitalization of down payments on AuC possible

Con:

  • Missing reference between document items on completed asset and items on WBS element
  • Lack of traceability during the settlement process

Recommendation

When to use summary settlement:

  • You want to settle to many receiver assets.
  • You want to settle amount based.
  • You want to capitalize down payments on an AuC
  • You have a high volume of postings on the investment project (WBS element).
  • The amount that you capitalize on the AuC is already aggregated (for example, by a settlement before) and must now be distributed to many asset receivers.

When to avoid summary settlement:

  • You have postings with a clear start-to-end relation and distribution to one or few receiver assets.

How to set up summary settlement

In Controlling, you create a WBS element with project type Investment Project. You assign an investment profile for summary settlement (for example, the default content profile Model with AuC, summary settlement (000001)).

When you release the investment project, a matching asset under construction is automatically created in Asset Accounting.

In Asset Accounting, you create the completed assets as receivers for the final AuC settlement.

Line Item-Based Settlement

There are two options for line item-based settlement:

  • Settlement of an independent AuC (in Asset Accounting only, that is, without Controlling integration) and
  • Event-based settlement (with Controlling integration)

Even though the two options differ greatly in the end, we want to describe the common characteristics first.

Characteristics

If the settlement is line item-based, each posting item is settled as a separate entity and distributed to the receivers according to the settlement rule. This enables the referencing of the predecessor document during settlement posting and thus the traceability of this document chain in analytical reporting.

On the other hand, this means that for many (N) postings that are to be settled to a large number (M) of receivers, each item is split to M receivers in this N:M relationship. This can quickly lead to a high volume of settlement items, each of which processes only a very small percentage of the item amount to be settled.

Example (where line item-based settlement is not recommended):

200 posting items on the AuC are assigned to a settlement rule with 120 receivers. This results in 24,000 retirement items and 24,000 acquisition items in the settlement document, that is per ledger and asset depreciation area. In addition, if there is an item with, for example, only 100 currency units to be settled, the distribution of these 100 currency units to 120 receivers may end for some receivers with 0 received currency units if the percentage per receiver is too small.

In this example, a line item-based settlement is not recommended. It is likely that performance issues occur.

The result is a very fine-granular split that does not make sense for the business process that line item-based settlement is supposed to support. Due to the high volume of settlement posting items and the split, the traceability advantage of line item-based settlement is not given anymore.

Figure 2 shows the negative effects of splitting to multiple receiver assets.

Figure 2: Line Item-Based Settlement of an AUC with N:M Relationship (not recommended)

Instead, line item-based settlement is suitable for projects with a small volume of journal entries and especially for a small number of settlement receivers per document item. Such a case is shown in Figure 3.

Figure 3: Line Item-Based Settlement of an AUC to a Single Receiver (recommended)

Example 1 (where line item-based settlement is recommended):

For a construction project, all expenses are posted to an AuC during the construction phase. The construction results in six different fixed assets (completed assets). Some of the expense items on the AuC can be assigned directly to an individual receiver asset. Few items shall be distributed to all six receiver assets. After the project is completed, the settlement is processed and the expenses are capitalized to the completed receiver assets corresponding to the assigned AuC items. In asset transaction reporting, the document chain can be traced back from each completed asset to the AuC posting item.

Line item-based settlement is particularly useful if the amounts are not aggregated at any point in the document chain, since the start-to-end relationship is clear and can be traced from the final asset acquisition posting back to the order item.

You can also use the line item-based settlement process, if you have normal asset purchases without construction process and the completed assets are not known or available at an early stage of the process.

Example 2 (where line item-based settlement is recommended):

For a purchase order of 40 laptops, you assign the amount of each laptop to the AuC as a separate order item. After the delivery of all 40 laptops, the goods receipt posting creates 40 items on the AuC – each with the individual amount of the laptop expense. For use within your organization, one fixed asset is created per laptop. When a laptop is handed out for use, the corresponding settlement item on the AuC can be assigned to the receiving asset, creating a 1:1 relationship between the AuC item created by the goods receipt posting and the settlement receiver. After settlement, you can trace the APC posting back to the goods receipt item and the order item for each receiving asset and for the AuC.

Pros and Cons

These are the pros and cons of line-item based settlement:

Pro:

  • Traceability from settlement receiver back to the settled item

Con:

  • Performance restrictions during the settlement of AuC to final receiver

Recommendation

When to use line-item based settlement:

  • Only a small number of items is to be settled per settlement.
  • An item to be settled typically refers to a specific receiver.
  • Different AuC items are to be settled to different receivers.

When to avoid line-item based settlement:

  • All items on the AuC typically are settled to the same set of many receivers, that means, the same (splitting) settlement rule is assigned to all items.
  • The items are to be settled to many receivers, for example, by a fixed portion
  • Many items are to be settled in the same settlement run.
  • Item amounts on the AuC are already aggregated and need to be split during the settlement.
  • The settlement rule contains amount-based distribution.

How to set up line item-based settlement

Line item-based settlement using an independent AuC (without Controlling integration):

You create an independent asset under construction (AuC) in Asset Accounting app Manage Fixed Assets (F3425A) using an asset class for AuC for which the Line Item Settlement indicator is set. Expenses can be posted directly to the AuC and appear there as items to be settled in app Define Distribution Rules for AuC (AIAB) where the settlement rules can be assigned and items can be settled.

Caution: The settlement of independent AuC can only be processed online and is not possible for scenarios with a mass split (described above).

Tip: If you are already using independent AuCs, here is how to avoid performance issues during settlement:

  • Avoid settling too many items in the same settlement. You can assign a distribution rule to only part of the items and then settle these items, then repeat with the distribution rule assignment and settlement of further items, and so on.
  • Perform the settlement separately for each ledger.

Note that activation of down payments is not possible for independent AuCs.

Note that the function of the independent AuC is to be deprecated in a future release.

Line item-based settlement using an investment project with event-based settlement

You can create a Project with the project type Investment Project and assign an investment profile for event-based settlement (for example, the default content profile Event-Based Model with AuC (EB0001)). As soon as you release the project, a corresponding asset under construction is created in Asset Accounting.

For each cost posting to the WBS element, the system settles the posting directly to the AuC. For this reason, there are line items on the AuC and it is possible to trace the document chain back from the cost posting to the AuC and to the final settlement receiver.

Therefore, it is important that this type of investment project and AuC are to be handled considering the pros and cons described in the above section about the line item-based approach.

Currently, it is not possible to assign a specific settlement rule directly to an AuC item, that means, all items on the AuC are settled with the settlement rule of the WBS element. In contrast to the summary settlement, however, the amounts on the AuC are not aggregated but get settled line item-wise. This means, if a complex distribution rule with many receivers is defined, the same negative effects of N:M splitting will occur.

Therefore, this type of investment project should only be used for settlement to a small number of settlement receivers, as described in the examples above.

Conclusion and More Information

The decision ultimately depends on your project's complexity and volume. For large-scale projects with many postings and receivers, summary settlement offers the performance you need. For smaller projects where tracking individual items matters, line item-based settlement provides the transparency you're looking for.

The right approach isn't about which is "better" - it's about which fits your specific business scenario. Take time to evaluate your project characteristics before making your choice.

Want to learn more about the settlement of assets under construction? - For more information, see the help portal documentation under Assets Under Construction: Management and Settlement, and Event-Based Settlement.

entry.title: Re: partitioning recommendation for the acctcr table
entry.content:
Hi, 
 
Customers usually use time based columns like YEAR, MONTH, DATE for RANGE partitioning since these type of columns are easy to define ranges for.
 
Depending on the column and the type of data, you need to first ensure that the number of records between each defined range of values per partition is not near or above the threshold of 2.14 billion.
 
So checking the record count for example witha query like this will show :
 
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ACCTCR WHERE <PARTITION COLUMN> BETWEEN 'X' AND 'Y'
 
and then define the RANGE partitions :
 
ALTER TABLE <SCHEMA_NAME>.<TABLE_NAME> PARTITION BY RANGE (c) 
         (PARTITION 1 <= VALUES < 5,
         PARTITION 5 <= VALUES < 10,
. . . .
         PARTITION OTHERS) ONLINE;
 
 
Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: Poland e-invoicing: SAP Concur Expense with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition
entry.content:
Poland e-invoicing: SAP Concur Expense with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition

Poland’s National e‑Invoicing System (KSeF) requires businesses to receive B2B invoices in a structured electronic format. To support this legal requirement for employee expenses, SAP Concur Expense integrates with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

The integration provides a compliant, automated, and user‑friendly way to receive Polish e‑invoices from KSeF and convert eligible invoices into employee expense items.

This document explains how the solution works and provides a high‑level overview of the required configuration.

Solution Overview

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition connects directly to the Polish tax authority platform KSeF (National e‑Invoice System) and retrieves electronic invoices issued to the company. It then:

  • Identifies Travel & Expense (T&E) invoices
  • Routes eligible invoices automatically to SAP Concur Expense

SAP Concur Expense then:

  • Identifies Concur user from employee information in invoice
  • Creates expense items with pre‑populated invoice data and link the invoice retrieved from KSeF to the expense created.

The solution uses employee information provided in Podmiot 3 – Role 11, as recommended by Polish authorities. Employee information in Podmiot 3, Role 11 is used to:

  1. Identify Travel & Expense (T&E) invoices
  2. Find relevant Concur user using Employee Email ID or Employee ID to create expense for the user.

This ensures accurate routing and assignment of expenses without manual intervention.

Business Benefits

  • Supports Poland’s KSeF e‑Invoicing mandate with native invoice retrieval and compliant document handling.
  • Employee‑related invoices are automatically identified and routed using employee information in Podmiot 3 – Role 11, eliminating manual classification.
  • Expense items are created automatically with pre‑populated data, eliminating need of manual expense creation.
  • Direct use of structured e‑invoice data minimizes errors and ensures consistent expense data.
  • Each expense retains the official KSeF invoice reference, supporting tax audits and compliance reporting.
  • Leverages SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition as a central compliance hub, helping organizations adapt to future e‑invoicing regulations.

Configuration Overview and Setup Guidance

The setup requires configuration in both SAP Document and Reporting Compliance and SAP Concur Expense.

1. Set Up Connectivity Between SAP Concur Expense and SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

  • Perform the initial steps in SAP BTP to provision SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.
  • Ensure you have an existing SAP Concur Expense account with the relevant administrator permissions.
  • Establish the connection between SAP Concur Expense account and SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition using the SAP Concur App Center.

 

Setting Up Connectivity to SAP Concur Expense (Country/Region Specifics)
https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/setting-up-connectivity-concur-expense-country-region-specifics

2. Configure Poland Process in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition

Activate and configure the Poland‑specific inbound e‑invoicing process in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

Configuring a Process Delivered by the Cloud Edition
https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/configuring-process-delivered-by-cloud-edition

As part of this step:

  • Configure routing rules based on Party Role Code ‘11’
  • Maintain the Concur destination (created during connectivity setup) as the target business system

 

Additional details on country‑specific rule parameters are available here:
https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/country-region-specific-parameters-for-rule-conditions

3. Configure Expense Group in SAP Concur Expense

  • Enable Poland as a digital compliance country
  • Assign the configuration at the appropriate Expense Group level

 


Configuration – Expense | SAP Help Portal
https://help.sap.com/docs/CONCUR_EXPENSE

End User Experience:

Employees can see an expense report created automatically with pre‑populated data, including:

  • Invoice ID
  • Merchant Tax ID
  • Government Invoice ID (KSeF ID)
  • The original KSeF e‑invoice and a human‑readable PDF linked to the expense

This significantly reduces effort for employees while ensuring data accuracy and compliance.

Summary

By integrating SAP Concur Expense with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, organizations can meet Poland’s KSeF requirements while simplifying employee expense processing.

entry.title: Re: SAP B1 GRPO posting wrong amount to Inventory acct fo...
entry.content: Hi Matija, ah I see what you are getting at. 1st GRPO price is $21,000. Cost calculated by SAP is 21,000. 2nd GRPO price is $22,500. Cost calculated by SAP is ($21,000 + $22,500) / 2 qty = $21,750. 3rd GRPO price is also $22,500. Cost calculated by SAP is ($21,000 + $22,500 + $22,500) / 3 qty = $22,000. So because there are 2 qty in SAP now, SAP takes $22,000 * 2 qty to get $44,000. Expecting $44,000 to be the total value of the item in SAP, and since the 2nd GRPO cost was adjusted to $21,750, SAP calculates that the 3rd GRPO cost should be adjusted to $44,000 - $21,750 = $22,250. Thank you for your help!

entry.title: Why Business Rules Become Unmanageable After Global Template Rollouts
entry.content:
Most SAP SuccessFactors implementations start with a simple goal: standardize global HR processes.
But at scale, that simplicity starts to break.

The Real Problem Isn’t Rules. It’s Scale.

Global template rollouts in SAP SuccessFactors are built on one promise:

  • One global design
  • One Employee Central template
  • Standardized HR processes

At design time, this works.

At scale, it doesn’t.

Business rules don’t break during implementation—they break during global expansion.

What starts as a clean rule framework slowly evolves into a fragmented, high-risk logic system.

The Logic Breakdown (Cause → Effect Model)

Step 1: Standardization Assumption

At the beginning, teams believe:

“We can create global rules and reuse them across all countries.”

Why this seems logical:

  • HR processes appear similar globally
  • EC data model is standardized
  • Rule framework is reusable

👉But this assumption ignores one thing: variation is inevitable

Step 2: Variation Enters the System

As rollout begins, countries introduce:

  • statutory requirements
  • local validations
  • unique job structures
  • payroll dependencies

Now the system must handle:

Global logic + Local exceptions

Step 3: The First Design Compromise

Instead of redesigning for flexibility, teams choose speed:

  • Clone existing rules
  • Add country-specific IF conditions
  • Patch exceptions into existing logic

👉This is the critical turning point

Step 4: Rule Duplication Begins

A single rule becomes multiple versions:

  • Global Rule
  • US Rule
  • Germany Rule
  • India Rule
  • APAC Variant

Why duplication happens:

  • Faster than redesign
  • Safer under deadlines
  • Easier to test locally

👉Effect: Logic is no longer centralized

Step 5: Rules Become Decision Trees

As more countries are added:

IF country = US → logic A 
IF country = DE → logic B 
IF country = IN → logic C 
ELSE → global logic

What changes:

  • Rules become longer
  • Conditions become nested
  • Readability drops

👉Effect: Rules stop being “logic” and become mini-programs

Step 6: Hidden Dependencies Emerge

Rules start interacting:

  • Rule A updates a field
  • Rule B reacts to that field
  • Rule C validates again

But no one designs this interaction intentionally.

👉Effect:

  • Unpredictable outcomes
  • Debugging becomes guesswork
  • Changes create side effects

Step 7: Governance Breaks Down

After go-live:

  • Multiple teams modify rules
  • Documentation becomes outdated
  • Ownership is unclear

Now:

  • HR modifies validations
  • IT adjusts integrations
  • Consultants patch issues

👉Effect: No single source of truth for logic

 

Step 8: Testing Complexity Explodes

With:

  • country variations
  • employee types
  • lifecycle events

The number of test scenarios grows exponentially.

👉Teams adapt by:

  • testing only main flows
  • ignoring edge cases

👉Effect: Issues move into production

 

Step 9: System Degradation (Final State)

At this stage:

You see:

  • 100–200+ rules
  • duplicated logic
  • conflicting outputs
  • slow transactions

You hear:

  • “Which rule is controlling this?”
  • “Why did this value change?”
  • “It works for US but not for Germany”

👉The system is still running—but no longer predictable

🎯The Real Root Cause

It’s not:

  • bad rules
  • bad consultants
  • bad tools

It is this assumption:

Standardization eliminates the need for flexibility design

That assumption is wrong.

✅What Should Have Happened Instead

Mature implementations design for variation from Day 1:

✔ 1. Separate Logic from Variation

  • Keep rules stable
  • Move country differences to parameters (MDF / tables)

✔ 2. Design “Variation Points”

Instead of:

  • embedding country logic inside rules

Design:

  • configurable inputs that drive behavior

✔ 3. Avoid Rule Cloning

  • One rule → multiple inputs
    NOT
  • multiple rules → same logic

✔ 4. Establish Rule Ownership

Define:

  • who creates rules
  • who modifies them
  • who retires them

✔ 5. Treat Rules as Architecture (Not Configuration)

  • version control mindset
  • periodic cleanup cycles
  • dependency awareness

Conclusion — The Core Insight

Business rules don’t become unmanageable because they were poorly designed.

They become unmanageable because:

they were not designed to handle controlled variation at scale

Final Thought

A successful global template is not one that enforces sameness.

It is one that: absorbs differences without multiplying logic

 Hi Community, 

Excited to dive into what’s coming next for integration, AI, and real-time enterprise architecture at SAP Sapphire 2026. This year’s sessions clearly show how integration is evolving from a backend capability into a strategic enabler for AI-driven business. 

Don’t miss the opportunity to gain insights, learn from customer success stories, and experience how integration is becoming the backbone for AI innovation.  

Join us at SAP Sapphire 2026! SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando | SAP Sapphire Virtual | SAP Sapphire Madrid 

Save the dates and register today.  

🚨 Stay tuned for session highlights and updates throughout the event! Explore Integration Suite Catalogue here! 

SAP Integration Suite empowers organizations to connect applications, data, and processes across hybrid landscapes while enabling AI-driven innovation. From prebuilt integrations to event-driven architectures and governance for AI agents, this track highlights how integration is evolving into a strategic enabler for intelligent enterprises. Take a look at our SAP Integration Suite sessions and demos👇 

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: Product Sessions

In these sessions, leading organizations share how they are leveraging SAP Integration Suite to solve real-world challenges. From enabling real-time commerce to scaling event-driven architectures and building AI-ready integration foundations, these stories provide practical insights into how integration drives business transformation across industries.

Leading organizations will share how they are leveraging SAP Integration Suite to solve real-world challenges. From enabling real-time commerce to scaling event-driven architectures and building AI-ready integration foundations, these stories provide practical insights into how integration drives business transformation across industries:

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: Demo Stations

The demo stations offer a hands-on look at how SAP Integration Suite capabilities come to life in practice. These sessions will showcase how to modernize integrations for AI and implement governance for AI-driven scenarios, giving a closer view of tools and approaches that can be directly applied in enterprise landscapes. 

BTP2137: Modernize Integrations for AI

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: ASUG Pre Conference Seminar

Join us for a 6-hour hands-on workshop designed to equip integration professionals with practical skills to modernize enterprise integrations on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). The session covers strategic integration in the AI era and includes real-world scenarios using Edge Integration Cell, focusing on ground-to-ground integration patterns and accelerating B2B integration capabilities. 

ASUG106: Cloud-Native Integration Mastery: From Strategy to AI-Driven Transformation — A Hands-On Deep Dive 

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: ASUG Preer Insights

This session highlights the SAP Customer Influence site as a transparent, AI-powered platform for submitting, tracking, and prioritizing ideas that shape the future of SAP Integration Suite. It enables customers to share real-time feedback, influence product roadmaps, and gain visibility into how other organizations are driving integration-led transformation across their landscapes. 

BTP1606: Customer Influence and feedback: Influence SAP Integration Suite  

 

Explore these sessions and plan your agenda to get the most out of SAP Sapphire 2026. Don’t miss out!

entry.title: *BCK - The Silver Lining : Rescuing Your FBL1N Selection Screen in S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Introduction - During the SAP ECC era, index tables like BSIK, BSAK, BSAD, BSID etc. were transparent tables, making them straightforward to extend directly. However, in S/4HANA, the architecture changed. These tables have been converted into Core Data Services (CDS) views built on top of BKPF and BSEG. Because of various system constraints (outlined in SAP Note 3170581), developers can no longer extend these views directly.

So, how do we move forward?

*The Silver Lining:  With the introduction of S/4HANA, SAP provided additional tables such as BSIS_BCK, BSAS_BCK, BSIK_BCK, and BSAK_BCK (Accounting: Secondary Index for G/L, Vendor, Customer Accounts - Back-up)

These are part of the Compatibility Views and are technical structures primarily used for data migration and archiving from ECC systems. While new S/4HANA postings do not update these specific tables, the core CDS views (like BSIK and BSAK) are linked to them. This linkage is the key to our solution: if we can add new fields to these *BCK tables, the fields will subsequently reflect in the core CDS views

Prerequisites for Extension -

  1. The extended fields you want to add must already exist in either the BKPF or BSEG tables.
  2. Both the open items table (BSIK_BCK) and the cleared items table (BSAK_BCK) must be extended with the exact same fields in the exact same order.

Step-by-Step Technical Guide ( Environment - S/4 HANA OP 2025 )

Step 1: Append Structures to the Backup Tables First, you need to create an append structure to Table BSIK_BCK. Then, repeat this process by adding an identical append structure to Table BSAK_BCK.

 

Append structure to Table BSAK_BCK

 

Note: Simply appending the structure to the *BCK tables will not immediately extend the BSIK view.

BSIK is not extended yet

Step 2: Regenerate the CDS Views To push the newly appended fields into the main views, you must execute a specific report.

Run the ABAP report: FGL_MIG_REGENERATE_CDS_GL.

Executing this report will regenerate the views of the index tables (BSIK, BSAK, BSAD, BSID, etc.) and successfully add the new fields from the BSIK_BCK append structure into the main BSIK view.

The fields have been added to the view BSIK

Step 3: Update the Logical Database for FBL1N Now that the views are extended, the final phase is to expose these fields on the FBL1N selection screen, referencing SAP Note 3455167.

Go to transaction SE36.

Select Logical Database KDF.

Navigate to Extras -> Selection View, and choose Copy to CUS (Customer space).

Add Group 04 and label it as 'Additional Selection'.

Assign Group ID 04 to your newly extended fields.

Once these steps are completed and saved, your custom fields will successfully appear in the FBL1N selection screen, ready for business users to leverage.

  • The same concept should work across supplier, customer and GL line report ( FBL1N, FBL5N, FBL3N ).
  • While SAP recommends to use equivalent Fiori app (F0712 – Manage supplier line item in this case) which can be extended using ‘custom fields and logic’ app, there is still wide usage of FBL1N among business users.

Conclusion - The underlying business requirement to enhance FBL1N screen remains a driver for business user to work efficiently and same is achieved in S/4 using the steps mentioned in this blog post.

entry.title: SAP ByDesign ManageEmailActivityIn
entry.content:
Hi,

In SAP Business ByDesign I'm trying to make a solution which automatically sends out an email to a party linked to the sales order when it's released. I've been told that the EmailActivity object is deprecated (though the repository explorer says otherwise) and that the Activity object should be used instead, but that has no available Create() action. The EmailActivity object is successfully creating, but no email is getting sent. It's not the system email setup at fault (that I'm aware of) as sending the test email from the business configuration email settings works.

I've also tried using the ManageEmailActivityIn web service, both in SoapUI and as a WSID file in the solution. It always seems to complain that the sender must be the logged-in user, even when I provide the MessageFromParty.PartyKey.PartyID.content element the business partner internal ID of the currently logged-in user from Identity.Person.InternalID, using Context.GetCurrentUserIdentityUUID() to retrieve the appropriate instance. The only time it doesn't complain about it is when I set the MessageFrom BP to the same as the MessageTo BP but that still doesn't send anything.

So my question is: has anyone recently had any success doing something like this in ByDesign? See below an example of the embedded WSID code I'm using.

var identity = Identity.Retrieve(Context.GetCurrentIdentityUUID());
var request: ManageEmailActivityInWSID.MaintainBundle.Request;
var sync: ManageEmailActivityInWSID.MaintainBundle.Request.EmailActivityBundleMaintainRequest_sync_V1;
var inst: ManageEmailActivityInWSID.MaintainBundle.Request.EmailActivityBundleMaintainRequest_sync_V1.EmailActivity;
inst.actionCode = "01";
inst.InformationSensitivityCode = "1";
inst.MessageFromParty.BusinessPartnerInternalID = identity.Person.InternalID.RemoveLeadingZeros();
inst.EmployeeResponsibleParty.BusinessPartnerInternalID = inst.MessageFromParty.BusinessPartnerInternalID;
var to: ManageEmailActivityInWSID.MaintainBundle.Request.EmailActivityBundleMaintainRequest_sync_V1.EmailActivity.MessageToParty;
to.BusinessPartnerInternalID = "1002656";
inst.MessageToParty.Add(to);
inst.Name = "Hello";
var text: ManageEmailActivityInWSID.MaintainBundle.Request.EmailActivityBundleMaintainRequest_sync_V1.EmailActivity.Text;
text.actionCode = "01";
text.ContentText = "Hello world.";
text.TextTypeCode.content = "10002";
inst.Text.Add(text);
sync.EmailActivity.Add(inst);
request.EmailActivityBundleMaintainRequest_sync_V1 = sync;
var response = ManageEmailActivityInWSID.MaintainBundle(request, "", "EmailCSD");

Thanks,

Lewis

entry.title: Error: FBV0: No batch input data found for dynpro SAPMF05A 0701. SAPMF05A 0330 expected
entry.content:
Issue Summary

The document is parked successfully in FB01.
As per standard SAP behavior, in a cross‑company code scenario, automatic postings are created for the respective company codes.


During parking, the system resets certain fields.


At the time of posting via FBV0, the document misses mandatory data (SCB Indicator) for Company Code country‑specific requirement).


Due to this, FBV0 posting fails.
Proposed Options


Option 1 – Workaround
Perform FBV0 posting via Batch Input in foreground using SM35.
The system will stop at each screen, allowing validation of mandatory fields.
SCB Indicator can be filled during processing.

Option 2
Create two separate documents, one for each company code, and perform park & post individually.

Option 3 – System Enhancement
Explore enhancement to automatically populate SCB Indicator during posting.

this is difficult to answer per se. Each app performs different restriction checks during usage of the app.

entry.title: Re: MSR_D_UPDATED table how its updated. ARM Refund contr...
entry.content:
SAP does not recommend directly updating the MSR_D_UPDATED table at runtime. Instead, you should use the Advanced Returns Management (ARM) framework and available BADIs to influence how fields like Refund_control and Refund_type are determined during return order processing

For information, Refund_control and Refund_type are updated when a return order is processed and follow-up activities (refund, replacement, inspection, etc.) are triggered and not meant for direct custom writes.

entry.title: Re: Sap exam preparation
entry.content:
Hi,

I understand it is frustrating to not pass the exam and not knowing exactly where you are failing, this is the current design and it is to a degree needed as you can take the exam again multiple times. 

I think what you are confusing is task completion vs. task correctness: it's not enough to complete a task without errors on screen. The system evaluates whether the end result/output matches the expected outcome exactly. For example, if you configured a document type but used the wrong number range or missed a field status variant setting, the task may be marked incorrect even though no error message appeared. The evaluation is automated based on the expected outcome and everything matters, to be honest "almost" correct is not good enough to score points (just as before with the question based exams you would score no points for partially correct answers).

After taking the exam, you should have access to the pdf with all the tasks, I would really read through them, potentially book a system and go through them without the time-pressure and stress of taking an exam. I am sure you will figure out what you are missing this way.

entry.title: SAP Customer Checkout, cloud edition 3.0 2602 (Released on 20-April-2026)
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce that SAP Customer Checkout, cloud edition 3.0 Feature Pack 2602 has been released on the 20th of April 2026.

This new release 2602 includes the following highlights:

  • Migration from On-premise Edition to Cloud Edition
    • Starting with SAP Customer Checkout 2.0 Feature Pack 21 PL24 (on-premise edition), a new migration tool will be available in the tools folder of SAP Customer Checkout 2.0 Feature Pack 21 PL24, onwards. This tool allows you to easily migrate key business data (such as Articles, Article Groups, Organizational Structure, Price Lists, Roles, Users, and Gift Cards) from your on-premise SAP Customer Checkout manager system to the SAP Customer Checkout manager, cloud edition system
    • New communication support is available in SAP Customer Checkout manager 2.0 Feature Pack 21 (on-premise edition) to connect to SAP Customer Checkout manager, cloud edition. This enables customers to operate on-premise and cloud systems in parallel and supports a seamless transition to cloud-based operations
    • Update from SAP Customer Checkout point-of-sale 2.0 FP21 version to SAP Customer Checkout point-of-sale, cloud edition 3.0 version is available. It is very important to note that the point-of-sale must be on SAP Customer Checkout point-of-sale 2.0 FP21 before you start the update process. If you update from an older version of point-of-sale, you will end up in an inconsistent state. In the SAP Customer Checkout manager 2.0 Feature Pack 21 (on-premise edition), in the Software app, it is possible to upload the update.zip file with SAP Customer Checkout point-of-sale, cloud edition 3.0 Feature pack 2602, and integrate it with a new SAP Customer Checkout manager, cloud edition version. This update introduces a new property file, "cloud_upgrade.properties", to help with the cloud upgrade process
  • Self-Checkout User interface
    • The Support menu has been enhanced with detailed software information to improve customer support efficiency
    • New license check dialog is now available to notify users if the POS is unlicensed or if the license is revoked during the sales process
    • New “Customer and Loyalty” configuration enables user interface customization and provides the ability to apply customer-specific and loyalty-related benefits and discounts
    • Under the Users app, a new permission, "Self-Checkout: Scan of Unknown IDs," has been added. This allows you to control whether a Self-Checkout user can scan unknown IDs or require additional supervisor authentication
    • New Multi-Object Dialog helps identify which object the scanned ID belongs to (e.g., customer or article), allowing the user to select the correct option
    • New “Plugin” scan setting allows integration of plugins with the Self-Checkout system
    • An article image added to the articles in the SAP Customer Checkout manager, cloud edition, is also shown in the Self-Checkout
  • French Certification
    • A new "Fiscal Categories" configuration has been added, tailored for taxes and regulations in France and its territories. This update simplifies tax analysis and reconciliation, improving reporting accuracy
  • Local Tax Calculation Mode for the United States of America (US)
    • A new payment authorization feature allows cashiers to authorize a payment amount using a dedicated button and complete the final capture later, including the option to add a tip   
  • Receipt export job is improved with a new format “Excel (Sales item level)” that tracks/exports sales data at the item level.
  • New event group “User authorization” available in the Monitoring app helping you monitor permission approvals and track sensitive POS operations performed by super users, such as receipt returns carried out by cashiers with limited permissions. Records are stored for 30 days
  • New navigation mode is now available for Quick Selections, allowing users to navigate directly to a category or group. This feature is especially helpful for Quick Selections that contain many categories or groups, including those that are hidden, as well as for use on small devices or mobile phones
  • New Based on business date cashing-up calculation option is available that provides more flexible reporting options for locations with cashless operations, as well as for POS systems with automatic end-of-day closing settings
  • New button "Show QR Code” is implemented, it allows staff to display the receipt QR code on both the POS screen and the Customer Display, which customers can scan for quick access to their receipt
  • And many more features

    Hope you enjoyed the blog. Best of luck with your SAP Customer Checkout, cloud edition journey.

    Help: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CUSTOMER_CHECKOUT_CLOUD
    PartnerEdge https://partneredge.sap.com/en/products/sapcc/scccld/about.html

    Feel free to comment and give feedback about the blog.

    About me: 
    My name is Bikash Bansal. I work for SAP SE, Germany. My current role is of Product Owner for SAP Customer Checkout, cloud edition. You can find more blogs from me about SAP Customer Checkout here .

entry.title: Re: Release strategy for pricing condition record (MEK1,...
entry.content: Standard SAP does not trigger release strategy for condition record changes (MEK1/MEK2). The recommended approach is to implement a custom workflow or BRF+ rule to govern condition maintenance. Direct integration with PO release strategy is not supported.  You can explore a custom enhancement to monitor condition changes and trigger an approval process if required.

entry.title: Re: How to copy roh parts from one super bom to another w...
entry.content: To copy ROH parts from one super BOM to another with all dependencies, use CEWB or BOM Copy functions. For dependencies (object dependencies, classifications, documents), you’ll need to either configure CEWB to include them or build a custom program using BOM and dependency BAPIs.

entry.title: Re: Issue with release strategy
entry.content:
Which system you have done release strategy configuration set up and which system you are testing - share those details!

NOTE: In case of release strategy set up done in development system and TR transport to quality system- check release characteristics and release class created in quality system along with value object/s are mapped in CL20N for your release strategy and release class before testing in quality system
